## Question

What causes earthquakes?

response_text: ANSWER: Most earthquakes occur when stresses from the slow motion of Earth’s tectonic plates build up along faults until rocks suddenly slip, releasing stored elastic energy as seismic waves; this “stick-slip” behavior is common at plate boundaries—subduction zones, transform faults, and rift zones—but can also happen on faults within plates. The rupture starts at a point underground called the focus (or hypocenter) and the shaking is felt at the surface above the epicenter. Other, less common causes include volcanic activity (magma movement fracturing rock), landslides or cave/cavity collapses, and large meteor impacts. Human activities can also induce seismicity, such as fluid injection or withdrawal (e.g., wastewater disposal, geothermal operations), reservoir impoundment, mining, and, rarely, large explosions. Regardless of the trigger, earthquakes reflect a rapid release of strain that propagates through the crust as compressional and shear waves.
